questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q:
Assertion : When hot water is poured in a beaker of thick glass, the beaker cracks.
Reason : Outer surface of the beaker expands suddenly.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: When we pour hot water in beaker, the inner surface of the glass expands on heating. But heat from inside does not reach quickly the outer surface of the glass, because glass is a bad conductor of heat. Hence, the outer surface does not expand and the glass cracks.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q:
Assertion : In pressure-temperature $(P-T)$ phase diagram of water, the slope of the melting curve is found to be negative.
Reason : Ice contracts on melting to water.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: The negative slope is because of change of phase. This happens to liquids which contract on melting.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Two metal rods 1 and 2 of same lengths have same temperature difference between their ends. Their thermal conductivities are $K_1$ and $K_2$ and crosssectional areas $A_1$ and $A_2$ respectively. If the rate of heat conduction in 1 is four times that in 2 , then:
questions.solution:
Let $L=$ length of each rod Temperature difference $=\Delta T$
$\therefore$ Rate of flow of rod (i) is $H_1=$ $\frac{K_1 A_1 \Delta T}{L}$ and Rate of flow of rod (2) is $H_2=\frac{K_2 A_2 \Delta T}{L}$ from the equation
$\begin{array}{rlrl}
H_1 & =4 H_2 \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{K_1 A_1 \Delta \mathrm{T}}{L} & =4 \frac{K_2 A_2 \Delta T}{L} \\
\Rightarrow & K_1 A_1 & =4 K_2 A_2
\end{array}$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The two ends of a metal rod are maintained at temperatures <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100 </mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>110 </mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> . The rate of heat flow in the rod is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4.0</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mtext>J s</mtext><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . If the ends are maintained at temperatures <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200 </mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>210 </mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> , the rate of heat flow will be:
questions.solution:
In case I, temperature difference between the ends of rod is =110-100=10oC
In case II, temperature difference between the ends of rod is =210-200=10oC
Hence, in both the case temperature difference are same i.e. 10oC
∴ Rate of heat flow is also 4 J s-1 in both the cases.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q:
Assertion : It is hotter over the top of a fire than at the same distance on the sides.
Reason : Air surrounding the fire conducts more heat upwards.
options: if both assertion and reason are true and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
if both assertion and reason are true and the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
if assertion is true but reason is false
if both assertion and reason are false statements.
questions.solution: When we put a fire in a place, the air above the fire gets heated up. Hot air being light moves up. To fill up the gap cold air from the side of the fire rushes in. As the top of a fire is covered by hot air, it feels hotter over the top of the fire than at the same distance on the sides.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q:
Assertion : Water kept in an open vessel will quickly evaporate on the surface of the moon.
Reason : The temperature at the surface of the moon is much higher than boiling point of water.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Water would evaporate quickly because there is no atmosphere on moon, due to which surface temperature of moon is much higher than earth (maximum surface temperature of moon is $123^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ).
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Emissive and absorptive power of a material at $2000 \mathrm{~K}$ is 8 and 10 respectively, calculate the emissivity of IBB (Ideal black body)
questions.solution:
The ratio of emissive power to absorptive power is the same for all surfaces at the same temperature and is equal to the emissivity for a perfectly black body at that temperature.
$$
\text { i.e, } \frac{e}{a}=\varepsilon
$$
Here, $e=8, a=10$
$$
\therefore \quad \varepsilon=\frac{e}{a}=\frac{8}{10}=0.8
$$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: A clock pendulum having coefficient of linear expansion, α=9×10-7/Co has a period of 0.5s at 20oC. If the clock is used in a climate where the temperature is 30oC, how much time does the clock lose in each oscillation? g=constant
questions.solution:
∵dTT=12∙α∝dT
dT=12129×10-710
dT=2.25×10-6
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The cold junction of a thermocouple is at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The thermo e.m.f produced in the thermocouple is given by an equation $E=16 T-0.04 T^2$, where $T$ is the temperature of the hot junction. The temperature of inversion and neutral temperature of the thermocouple are
questions.solution:
Thermo emf $E=16 T-0.04 T^2$
At inversion temperature $\left(T i\right), E=0$
$$
\therefore \quad \begin{aligned}
0 & =16 T_i-0.04 T_i^2 \\
16 T i & =0.04 T_i^2 \\
T i & =\frac{16}{0.04}=400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}
$$
Neutral temperature $T_n=\frac{T i+T}{2}$
$T=$ Temperature of cold end
$$
T_n=\frac{400+0}{2}=200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}
$$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: A deep rectangular pond of surface area $\mathrm{A}$, containing water (density $=\rho$, specific heat capacity $=s$ ), is located in a region where the outside air temperature is a steady value at the $-26^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The thickness of the frozen ice layer in this pond, at a certain instant is $\mathrm{x}$. Taking the thermal conductivity of ice as $\mathrm{K}$, and its specific latent heat of fusion as L, the rate of increase of the thickness of ice layer, at this instant would be given by
questions.solution:
Key Idea If area of cross-section of a surface is not uniform or if the steady state condition is not reached, the heat flow equation can be applied to a thin layer of material perpendicular to direction of heat flow.
The rate of heat flow by conduction for growth of ice is given by,
$\frac{d \theta}{d t}=\frac{\left.K A\left(\theta_0-\theta_0\right)\right]}{x}$
where, $d \theta=\rho A d x L, \theta_0=0$ and $\theta_1=-\theta$
Given, $\theta_0=0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \theta_1=-26^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
The rate of increase of thickness can be calculated from Eq.
$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}} & =\frac{\mathrm{KA}\left(\theta_0-\theta_1\right)}{\mathrm{x}} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\rho \mathrm{AdxL}}{\mathrm{dt}} & =\frac{\mathrm{KA}\left(\theta_0-\theta_1\right)}{\mathrm{x}} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}} & =\frac{\mathrm{KA}\left(\theta_0-\theta_1\right)}{\rho \mathrm{AxL}} \\
& =\frac{\mathrm{K}[0-(-26)]}{\rho \times \mathrm{L}}=\frac{26 \mathrm{~K}}{\rho \times \mathrm{L}}
\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Which of the following statements is true/correct?
options: During clear nights, the temperature rises steadily upward near the ground level
Newton's law of cooling, an approximate form of Stefan's law, is valid only for natural convection
The total energy emitted by a black body per unit time per unit area is proportional to the square of its temperature in the Kelvin scale

Two spheres of the same material have radii $1 \mathrm{~m}$ and $4 \mathrm{~m}$ and temperatures $4000 \mathrm{~K}$ and $2000 \mathrm{~K}$ respectively. The energy radiated per second by the first sphere is greater than that radiated per second by the second sphere
questions.solution:
During clear nights object on surface of earth radiate out heat and temperature falls. Hence option (a) is wrong. The total energy radiated by a body per unit time per unit area $E \propto T^{4}$. Hence option (c) is
wrong. Energy radiated per second is given by
$\begin{aligned} \frac{Q}{t} &=P A \varepsilon \sigma T^{4} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{P_{1}}{P_{2}} &=\frac{A_{1}}{A_{2}}\left(\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}\right)^{4}=\left(\frac{\pi}{r_{2}}\right)^{2} \cdot\left(\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}\right)^{4} \\ &=\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{2}\left(\frac{4000}{200}\right)=\frac{1}{1} \end{aligned}$
$\because P_{1}=P_{2}$, hence option (d) is wrong. Newton's law is an approximate from of Stefan's law of radiation and works well for natural convection. Hence option (b) is correct.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: A cylindrical metallic rod in thermal contact with two reservoirs of heat at its two end conduct an amount of heat ' $Q_1$ ' in time ' $t$ ' the metallic rod is melted and the material is formed into a rod of length four times the length of original rod. The amount of heat conducted by the new rod when placed in thermal contact with the same two reservoirs in time $t$ is ' $Q_2$ '. Then $\frac{Q_1}{Q_2}$ is
questions.solution:
The heat conduction rate is given by:
$\frac{Q}{t}=\frac{k A\left(T_1-T_2\right)}{l}$
Considering volume conservation:
$\begin{aligned} & A_1 l_1=A_2 l_2 \\ & A_1 l_1=A_2\left(4 l_1\right) \\ & \Rightarrow A_2=\frac{A_1}{4} \\ & \frac{Q_2}{t}=\frac{k\left(\frac{A_1}{4}\right)\left(T_1-T_2\right)}{4 l_1}=\frac{1}{16} \frac{k A_1\left(T_1-T_2\right)}{l_1}=\frac{1}{16} \frac{Q_1}{t} \\ & \Rightarrow Q_2=\frac{1}{16} Q_1\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Two metal slabs of same cross-sectional area have thicknesses $d_1$ and ; and thermal conductivities $K_1$ and $K_2$ respectively, are connected in series. The free ends of the two slabs are kept at temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2\left(T_1>T_2\right)$. The temperature $T$ of their common junction is
questions.solution:
Heat current, $\dot{Q}_1=\frac{K_1\left(T_1-T\right) A}{d_1}$
For second slab,
Heat current, $\dot{Q}_2=\frac{K_2\left(T-T_2\right) A}{d_2}$
As slabs are in series same heat current flows through them
$\dot{Q}_1=\dot{Q}_2$
$\therefore \frac{K_1\left(T_1-T\right) A}{d_1}=\frac{K_2\left(T-T_2\right) A}{d_2}$
Therefore, temperature $T$ of their common junction is
$T=\frac{K_1 T_1 d_2+K_2 T_2 d_1}{K_2 d_1+K_1 d_2}$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: $22 \mathrm{~g}$ of carbon dioxide at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is mixed in closed container with $16 \mathrm{~g}$ of oxygen at $37^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If both gases are considered as ideal gases, then the temperature of the mixture is nearly
questions.solution:
Let $T^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ be the temperature of the mixture.
heat given by $\mathrm{O}_2=$ heat absorbed by $\mathrm{CO}_2$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \mu_1 C_{V_1} \Delta T=\mu_2 C_{V_2} \Delta T \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{22}{44}(3 R)(T-27)=\frac{16}{32}\left(\frac{5}{2} R\right)(37-T) \\ & \Rightarrow 3(T-27)=\frac{5}{2}(37-T) \\ & \Rightarrow 6 T-162=185-5 T \\ & \Rightarrow 11 T=347 \\ & \Rightarrow T=31.5 \circ \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: A hot body placed in air cools down to a lower temperature. The rate of decrease of temperature is proportional to the temperature difference from the surrounding. The body loses $60 \%$ and $80 \%$ of maximum heat it can loose in time $t_1$ and $t_2$ respectively. The ratio $t_2 / t_1$ will be
questions.solution:
According to Newton's law of cooling,
$$
-\frac{d T}{d t} \alpha \Delta \mathrm{T} \text { or } \frac{d T}{d t}=-K \Delta \mathrm{T}
$$
Solved the above Eq., we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
T(t) & =T_0+\left(T_1-T_0\right) e^{-k t} \Rightarrow e^{-k t}=\frac{T(t)-T_0}{T_1-T_0} \\
\text { or } \quad t & =\frac{1}{k} \ln \frac{T_1-T_0}{T(t)-T_0}
\end{aligned}
$$
Where, $T_1=$ temperature of body at $t=0$
$T_0=$ temperature of surrounding.
Let $T_1=0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
So, $t=\frac{1}{k} \ln \frac{-T_0}{T(t)-T_0}=\frac{1}{k} \ln \frac{-Q_0}{Q(t)-Q_0}[\because Q(t)=m c T(t)]$
According to the question, the body loses $60 \%$ and $80 \%$ of maximum heat in time $t_1$ and $t_2$


$$
\text { then } \quad \begin{aligned}
t_1 & =\frac{1}{k} \ln \left(\frac{-Q_0}{\frac{60}{100} Q_0-Q_0}\right) \\
t_2 & =\frac{1}{k} \ln \left(\frac{-Q_0}{\frac{80}{100} Q_0-Q_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$
Divide Eqs. (ii) by (i), we get
$$
\frac{t_2}{t_1}=\frac{\frac{1}{k} \ln \left(\frac{-Q_0}{\frac{80}{100} Q_0-Q_0}\right)}{\frac{1}{k} \ln \left(\frac{-Q_0}{100-Q_0}\right)}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \frac{t_2}{t_1}=\frac{\ln \left(\frac{-Q_0}{\left.\frac{80 Q_0-100 Q_0}{100}\right)}\right.}{\ln \left(\frac{-Q_0}{\left.\frac{60 Q_0-100 Q_0}{100}\right)}\right.}=\frac{\ln \left(\frac{-Q_0 \times 100}{-20 Q_0}\right)}{\ln \left(\frac{Q_0 \times 5}{2 Q_0}\right)}
$$
Hence, the correct option is (4).
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The coefficients of apparent expansion of a liquid when determined using two different vessels $A$ and $B$ are $y_1$ and $y_2$, respectively. If the coefficient of linear expansion of the vessel $A$ is $\alpha$, the coefficient of linear expansion of the vessel $B$ is
questions.solution:
Coefficient of apparent expansion of liquid for vessel $(A)=\gamma_1$
Coefficient of apparent expansion of liquid for vessel $(B)=\gamma_2$
For vessel $A$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \gamma_{\text {real }}=\gamma_{\text {app }}+\gamma_{\text {vessel }} \\
& \text { For vessel } \quad \gamma_{\text {vessel }}=3 \alpha_A=3 \alpha \\
& \because \quad \gamma_{\text {real }}=\gamma_1+3 \alpha \\
& \text { For vessel } B, \quad \gamma_{\text {real }}=\gamma_2+3 \alpha_B \\
& \therefore \quad \gamma_1+3 \alpha=\gamma_2+3 \alpha_B \\
& \alpha_B=\frac{\gamma_1-\gamma_2+3 \alpha}{3}=\frac{\gamma_1-\gamma_2}{3}+\alpha \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: A solid cylinder of radius $r_1=2.5 \mathrm{~cm}$, length $l_1=5.0 \mathrm{~cm}$ and temperature $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is suspended in an environment of temperature $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The thermal radiation transfer rate for cylinder is $1.0 \mathrm{~W}$. If the cylinder is stretched until its radius becomes $r_2=0.50 \mathrm{~cm}$, the thermal radiation transfer rate is changed to
questions.solution:
Keeping nature of surface and temperature of body and surroundings same, rate of heat transfer by radiation depends on area of body,
$\Rightarrow$ Rate of heat transfer $\propto$ Surface area of body
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q_2}{Q_1}=\frac{S_2}{S_1} \Rightarrow Q_2=Q_1 \times \frac{S_2}{S_1}$ $\ldots(i)$
Now, volume remains same in stretching, so
$\pi r_1^2 l_1=\pi r_2^2 l_2$
$\Rightarrow \quad l_2=\frac{r_1^2}{r_2^2} \cdot l_1=\frac{(2.5)^2}{(0.5)^2} \times 5=125 \mathrm{~cm}$
So, area before stretching, $S_1=2 \pi r_1\left(l_1+r_1\right)$ and area after stretching, $S_2=2 \pi r_2\left(l_2+r_2\right)$ Hence by Eq. (i), we get
$Q_2=Q_1 \times \frac{S_2}{S_1}=Q_1 \times \frac{2 \pi r_2\left(l_2+r_2\right)}{2 \pi r_1\left(l_1+r_1\right)}$
$=Q_1 \times \frac{r_2\left(l_2+r_2\right)}{r_1\left(l_1+r_1\right)}=\frac{1 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-2}(125+0.5) \times 10^{-2}}{2.5 \times 10^{-2}(5+2.5) \times 10^{-2}}$
$=3.346=3.35 \mathrm{~W}$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: A metal rod $A B$ of length $10 x$ has its one end $A$ in ice at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and the other end $B$ in water at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If a point $P$ on the rod is maintained at $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, then it is found that equal amounts of water and ice evaporate and melt per unit time. The latent heat of evaporation of water is $540 \mathrm{calg}^{-1}$ and latent heat of melting of ice is $80 \mathrm{calg}^{-1}$. If the point $P$ is at a distance of $\lambda x$ from the ice end $A$, find the value of $\lambda$. [Neglect any heat loss to the surrounding.]
questions.solution: Heat will flow both sides from point $P$. $$ \begin{aligned} L_1 \frac{d m_1}{d t} & =\left(\frac{\text { Temperature difference }}{\text { Thermal resistance }}\right)_1 \\ & =\frac{400}{(\lambda x) / k A} \\ L_1 \frac{d m_2}{d t} & =\frac{400-100}{(100-\lambda) x / k A} \end{aligned} $$ In about two equations, $$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{d m_1}{d t}=\frac{d m_2}{d t} \quad \text { (given) } \\ & L_1=80 \mathrm{calg}^{-1} \text { and } L_2=540 \mathrm{calg}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ Solving these two equations we get $\lambda=9$.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> slides on a rough horizontal surface. If the speed of the block decreases from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> the thermal energy developed in the process is
questions.solution:
According to the energy conservation, the heat(thermal energy) produced is equivalent to the loss in kinetic energy of the block.

Thus, thermal energy, H=12mv12-v22

H=12100×10-3102-52

H=3.75 J
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Fahrenheit and Celsius are the two scales used for measuring temperature. If the numerical value of a temperature recorded in both the scales is found to be same, then what is the temperature?
questions.solution:
Relation between Fahrenheit and Celsius scale is 

°C-0°100=F-32180

Given that numerical value of a temperature recorded in both the scales are same. 

°C=F=x   [say]

⇒x100=x-32180

⇒180x=100x-3200

⇒80x=-3200

⇒x=-320080⇒x=-40°
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
